narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 50% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold we use to make these tough decisions. Looking at your profile, you ranked in the 2nd decile for Year 1 school grades and the 1st decile for Year 3—much lower than the typical student's 6th or 5th decile rankings—while your LSAT score of 30 and undergraduate GPA of 2.5 also fall below the averages of 37 and 3.2. Your middle family income is right around the norm, and as a full-time White male applicant, your demographics align closely with most in our dataset, where over 80% are White, about half are male, and nearly all plan full-time study. The model's decision was driven mostly by your Year 3 school decile, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile. Your LSAT score came next in influence, also nudging the odds toward a fail prediction, with your undergraduate GPA, family income, racial background, full-time status, and gender playing smaller but still contributing roles in that direction. None of these factors pulled strongly in favor of passing, which is why the overall risk appeared balanced at 50% but too high for approval. Compared to successful applicants, your academic rankings and test scores stand out as notably weaker, putting you below the middle of the pack where passers typically cluster. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic performance through better study habits or tutoring, as school grades like your deciles are key signals of bar readiness. If possible, retake the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, since it heavily sways these predictions. Building a stronger undergraduate record retrospectively isn't feasible, but demonstrating improvement in any recent coursework could help offset past lows. Your family income and demographics are solid baselines that don't need changing. With targeted preparation, you could shift these influences and tip future predictions toward passing the bar. Keep pushing—you've got the drive to turn this around.
